title: My Afflictive Remote
text: For a long time I wanted a big screen tv for my bedroom and specifically I had in mind that it be  a Samsung model. For quite some time now my mobile phone has been a Samsung brand and my experience with it couldn't be any better. I finally had my dream come true and I purchased a Samsung 40" tv. My joy quickly turned into sadness and frustration since the remote of this set is a bit too complicated, at least for me. This remote is big and has too many separate buttons to operate it's features and for me, at age 72 and living alone, I find it too complicated to use it. I don't understand why Samsung had to manufacture such complicated device. I have a 50" TV set of a different brand in my living room and the remote is fantastic. It is extremely easy to use. It is much smaller and very convenient, with a lot less buttons to be pressing. Don't get me wrong about the quality of my 40" Samsung. The picture, sound and colors are magnificent. Overall I'm quite impressed and satisfied with quality of this tv set. If it wasn't by the remote of this 40" tv set I would have given it 5 stars. Unfortunately this remote takes away the joy that I normally would feel by watching my 40" tv set. What I feel instead is frustration and disappointment and it makes me wish that I had bought my 40" tv set of the same brand as my 50".

title: Three times is not the charm.
text:
This is my third Samsung TV, all were the same size but the first two were very expensive at about $450 each. I had to have a 32" model even though larger TV's were much cheaper. The first two lasted fairly well and I liked those TV.s. This latest one was $180 at my local electronics store that everyone has heard of. I was shocked at the low cost but happy.

For some reason this TV crashes every day. I don't care for the new interface because the "Recent Channels" list went away so it's harder to navigate and is extremely slow even connected directly to WiFi.

Maybe I just don't understand the interface. The TV gets a great picture and I use a soundbar. All three didn't have good sound built in, in my opinion. I loved that I didn't pay a huge price for this last one but that's the only thing I like along with the 32" screen which is getting hard to find in a full HD TV these days.

Most other brands I've seen usually have more ports for expansion that don't require extra cables or adapters for connecting peripherals.

In my opinion Samsung should do better in a lot of ways than the competition. I don't have any experience with Samsung's larger screen models and just wouldn't pay that kind of money when the competition is so 
much cheaper. 

I liked my Samsung phone but it's not likely I'll be buying another Samsung TV unless I need another 32" model and can't find one from another maker. It seems 32" models won't be available for long from what I'm seeing in the market these days.

 Tha app set up is beyond terrible.  The Samsung smart app wants control over the T.V. The apps are on a different page than the home screen. The app bar on Home page won't hold all of the apps. You have to hit Home, go up to the app tab and then to the Apps page. Scroll down and over to find the app. Hopefully it loads.
Most of the time it's very slow. If I think it didn't receive the signal, I'll try again. Then it catches up to itself and then jump ahead, past what I wanted.  Sometimes the TV will freeze streaming.  I have OTA antenna and wifi. 
 Both signals are strong and stable. I've checked the connections,  the network,  the router and reset tv.
It's hard to explain the frustration after buying the more expensive set and having remorse for a worse set.
For me, Samsung has lost the customer service and satisfaction.  Sorry 😞

I've had this TV for a few months now and it's been a great addition to my home. The picture quality is sharp and vivid — colors pop without looking overdone, and the contrast is solid whether I'm watching sports, movies, or streaming shows.

Setup was straightforward. The smart TV interface is intuitive and I had Netflix, YouTube, and my other apps running within minutes. The remote is clean and simple — no hunting for buttons.

Sound is better than I expected for a flat panel, though I did eventually add a soundbar for movie nights. At 4o inches it fits the room perfectly without being overkill.

Build quality feels premium. Thin bezels, clean lines — it looks good on the wall or on a stand.

For the price, you're getting a lot of TV. I'd buy it again without hesitation.

title: Great Family Entertainment!
text:
I’ve been genuinely impressed with the 32" Class FHD Smart TV F6000F (2025)—it’s one of those rare “small TV” purchases that feels like a real upgrade in day-to-day use.

Picture quality is the immediate standout. The Full HD resolution looks crisp and clean, with great sharpness for streaming shows, sports, and YouTube. Colors appear lively without looking cartoonish, and the image holds up nicely even in brighter rooms. For a 32-inch screen, it delivers a surprisingly polished, premium-looking picture.

The smart TV experience is also a big win. Apps load quickly, navigation is straightforward, and everything feels smooth and modern—no clunky menus or frustrating lag. It’s the kind of interface that makes you actually want to use the built-in smart features instead of defaulting to an external streaming stick.

What I appreciate most is how well-balanced this model is. The size is perfect for a bedroom, office, dorm, or kitchen setup, but it doesn’t feel like a “budget compromise.” It’s easy to set up, easy to live with, and it consistently delivers a great viewing experience without requiring constant tweaking.

On the security side, the F6000F (2025) feels thoughtfully designed for everyday peace of mind. The TV’s smart features work in a way that doesn’t feel intrusive, and the settings make it easy to manage privacy-related options without digging through endless menus. I also like that it supports straightforward software updating, so you’re not stuck with a device that slowly becomes outdated or exposed over time. Overall, it gives that reassuring “set it up once and you’re covered” experience that you want from a connected home device.

The sound quality is another pleasant surprise for a 32-inch set. Dialogue comes through clearly, which makes a huge difference for movies and streaming series, and there’s enough fullness to keep everyday viewing from sounding thin or tinny. It also handles volume changes well—quiet scenes don’t disappear, and louder moments don’t turn into harsh, messy noise. For most rooms, it’s more than enjoyable on its own, and if you ever want to level up, it pairs nicely with a soundbar for an even bigger, more cinematic feel.


If you want a reliable, sharp-looking FHD smart TV in a 32-inch class—one that feels current, responsive, and genuinely enjoyable—the F6000F (2025) is an excellent pick.

title: Software needs help!
text: I have always purchased Samsung TV’s.  They have been dependable, reliable, and have great picture quality.  When I needed a new TV for my bedroom, I didn’t look any further than Samsung and purchased a 32” F6000F series.  The setup was easy, it has an excellent picture, and the size is perfect for my bedroom.  However, there is a very disappointing issue with the software.  It is extremely sluggish and at times can become completely unresponsive causing the TV to freeze up.  The apps load painfully slow, and the tv responds to the remote control very slowly.  In an effort to fix the problem, I have performed both soft and hard resets on the set, I have ran the Device Care routine, cleared the cache and cleared data, deleted unused apps, and updated the software to make sure that the latest firmware is installed.  These steps help to minor degree (it is still sluggish), but as I continue to use the set, it gradually becomes more and more unresponsive.  A soft reset with an occasional hard reset is the quick “fix,” but it is a temporary fix.  I really hope that Samsung will release a software update to resolve this issue.  It is so frustrating to perform the simplest of functions, even like changing the volume, and have to deal with the wait time before the set will respond.

text:
Replaced my Samsung 32" with a new Samsung 32", Model UN32F6000. Very disappointed with several changes.
1. Closed Caption font is very small on most live broadcasts. There is no way to increase font size, which is odd. Accessibility is important and this feature helps!
2.Just accessing and activating Closed Captions is a chore. Instead of a single tap on a button to toggle it on/off, one must tap the cc button, wait for screen to come up LISTEN TO A VOICE READ THE COMMANDS, then toggle the cc on/off, all while missing whatever is on the screen.
3. There is no longer an alarm feature. I used to use the TV as an alarm clock, going on at a chosen time. It no longer does that! There is a sleep feature to turn the TV off after a set length of time, but not the alarm. WHY???
4. Despite seemingly turning off the audio reading of commands, it still does it. Annoying to have what is on the command screens read aloud if not needed!
Do not know why Samsung makes new TVs worse than the previous models.

The Samsung F6000F FHD TV (2025) is a budget-friendly option for those seeking a reliable Full HD experience. Priced affordably, this 40-inch model (UN40F6000FFXZA) delivers crisp 1080p resolution, ideal for casual viewing of movies, sports, and gaming. The vibrant display, showcased in the colorful promotional image, highlights decent color accuracy and contrast, though it lacks the depth of higher-end 4K models. The slim design fits well in smaller spaces, and its 2025 refresh suggests minor updates to connectivity, likely including HDMI and USB ports, though specifics aren’t detailed.
Performance is solid for the price, with Samsung’s HyperReal Engine enhancing picture quality. However, it misses advanced features like HDR or smart AI enhancements seen in premium TVs, such as Google’s AI-driven upscaling on newer models. Audio is adequate with built-in speakers, but a soundbar is recommended for immersive sound. The interface appears user-friendly, as seen on the experience.samsung.com setup page, though it’s unclear if it includes the latest Tizen OS with Google AI integration. With a seven-year update promise, it’s a durable choice, but its niche appeal suits budget-conscious buyers over tech enthusiasts.

title: Remote is useless for over-the-air channels
text: Generally, this TV is decent enough. As others have stated, it is very slow to respond to commands from the remote control. And its connectivity options are extremely limited. However, the big problem is that the particular remote control included with this TV does not have a "-" (dash) button, which is necessary to directly tune over-the-air channels if you are using an antenna. Over-the-air channels have a format of a number followed by a dash followed by another number. Without the dash button, you cannot key in a specific over-the-air channel number. Instead of having a dash button where every other Samsung remote control has one, which is to the left of the zero on the numeric keypad, it has a strange button labeled "TTX/MIX," which (whatever that is) is a feature that this TV does not even have. I called Samsung support to try to get them to send me a remote which would actually work, but they would not do so. So, if you plan on viewing over-the-air broadcast channels, either do not buy this TV or plan on spending more money to buy a different remote control that will actually work with your antenna.

title: A nice kitchen set, not designed for home theater.
text: Very good set for the kitchen wall, replacing an 8-year old Samsung 32-incher (UN32J5500AFXZA).  That one is working fine, but I wanted Bluetooth and the price on this 2025 model was certainly competitive. With the mount already in place, installation was a 5-minute job for my wife and me.  Performance so far (three days) is excellent, but there are a few shortcomings...NOT dealbreakers.  First, viewing angle is critical; the further my eyes deviate from perpendicular to the screen, the more washed-out the colors get...overly white and harsh, and surprisingly and significantly noticeable at about 60 degrees off perpendicular.  Second, I'm disappointed that there is no Intelligent Mode...we're used to having that on a couple of our other Samsung TV's (24" thru 75"). Third, and this may be something I can try to correct with live-chat tech support from Samsung at a later date, there's a noticeable time lag between the visual seen by my eyes and the audio received via Bluetooth by my ears. This may be no more than a half-second but it sure is user-hostile when the news or sports announcer's audio arrives after the lips have formed the sounds, or when the building blows up in silence with the sound of the explosion following shortly afterward.

title: 32" Class FHD Smart TV F6000F (2025)
text:
I recently purchased this Samsung 32-inch TV, and I couldn't be more pleased with its performance. This model features Smart View, allowing seamless connectivity and easy access to my favorite streaming services. According to the specs, it also supports Apple AirPlay, which enhances its versatility for all my devices. I love that it delivers Full HD quality. The lifelike colors make every viewing experience truly immersive, while the 3 Bezel-less design adds a modern touch to my entertainment setup. I also appreciate that it has a detachable power cord, making setup and cable management more convenient.

One suggestion for improvement would be to incorporate a plug-in leg design, where the legs can be attached without screws for easier assembly and a cleaner look. Overall, this TV is a fantastic addition to my home!
